name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَأْكُلُوٓا۟ أَمْوَٰلَكُم بَيْنَكُم بِٱلْبَـٰطِلِ إِلَّآ أَن تَكُونَ تِجَـٰرَةً عَن تَرَاضٍ مِّنكُمْ ۚ وَلَا تَقْتُلُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَكُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ بِكُمْ رَحِيمًا (٢٩)
```

```english
O you who have faith! Do not eat up your wealth among yourselves unrightfully, but it should be trade by mutual consent. And do not kill yourselves. Indeed Allah is most merciful to you. (29)
```

```arabic
وَمَن يَفْعَلْ ذَٰلِكَ عُدْوَٰنًا وَظُلْمًا فَسَوْفَ نُصْلِيهِ نَارًا ۚ وَكَانَ ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ يَسِيرًا (٣٠)
```

```english
And whoever does that in aggression and injustice, We will soon make him enter the Fire, and that is easy for Allah. (30)
```

## (Commentary)

In this verse, there is a semblance of connection with what preceded it, as it contains a prohibition against consuming wealth wrongfully (بالباطل), while the previous verses contained a prohibition against consuming the dowries of women through constraint and transgression. Thus, in this verse, there is a transition from the specific to the general.

His saying, the Exalted: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَأْكُلُوا أَمْوالَكُمْ** (O you who have faith! Do not eat your wealth) until His saying: **مِنْكُمْ** (from you).

__________________

(1) Narrated in *Nikah al-Wasa'il*.

Eating (الأكل) is well-known; it is the exhaustion of that which can be used as nourishment by seizing and swallowing it, for example. Because it contains the meaning of domination (التسلط) and exhaustion (الإنفاد), it is said: "The fire ate the wood," likening the fire's destruction of the wood by burning it to the way eating exhausts food through consumption and swallowing. It is also said: "So-and-so ate the wealth," meaning he disposed of it by exercising authority over it. This is based on the consideration that the primary way a human disposes of things is by nourishing himself with them, as it is his most intense need for survival and his most pressing requirement. Therefore, disposal is called "eating," but not every disposal—rather, it is the disposal arising from a domination that severs the domination of others over the wealth through ownership (التملك) and the like, as if he exhausts it by extending his authority over it and disposing of it, just as eating exhausts food.

The "wrongful" (الباطل) among actions is that which does not comprise a sound, rational purpose (غرض صحيح عقلائي). "Trade" (التجارة) is the disposal of capital in pursuit of profit, as mentioned by al-Raghib in his *Mufradat*, where he said: "There is no word in their [the Arabs'] speech containing a *ta* followed by a *jim* other than this term." Thus, it applies to transactions of buying and selling.

In restricting His saying: **لا تَأْكُلُوا أَمْوالَكُمْ** (Do not eat your wealth) with His saying: **بَيْنَكُمْ** (among yourselves), which indicates a type of collective gathering over wealth and its occurrence in their midst, there is an intimation or indication that the prohibited "eating" is that which occurs by way of circulating it among them and transferring it from one to another through exchange and rotation. Thus, the sentence—meaning His saying: **لا تَأْكُلُوا أَمْوالَكُمْ بَيْنَكُمْ** (Do not eat your wealth among yourselves)—after being restricted by His saying: **بِالْباطِلِ** (wrongfully), signifies the prohibition of transferential transactions that do not lead society toward its felicity and success, but rather harm it and drag it toward corruption and destruction. These are the transactions that are void (باطلة) in the view of the religion, such as usury (الربا), gambling (القمار), and uncertain sales (البيوع الغررية) like "sale by the pebble" (البيع بالحصاة), "sale by the date-stone" (النواة), and the like.

Based on this, the exception occurring in His saying: **إِلَّا أَنْ تَكُونَ تِجارَةً عَنْ تَراضٍ مِنْكُمْ** (unless it be trade by mutual consent from you) is a "disjunctive exception" (استثناء منقطع) — *an exception where the excepted item does not belong to the same category as the general group mentioned before it* — brought to repel a potential objection. For when He prohibited consuming wealth wrongfully—and the types of transactions circulating in a corrupt society through which financial transfer and transition occur, such as usurious, uncertain, and gambling transactions and their likes, are void in the view of the Law—it was possible to imagine that this would lead to the collapse of the pillars of society and the disintegration of its parts, resulting in the destruction of the people. Thus, this was answered by mentioning a type of transaction capable of organizing the scattered elements of society, strengthening its backbone, and preserving its uprightness: namely, trade based on mutual consent and sound transaction that fulfills the needs of society. This is similar to His saying, the Exalted: **يَوْمَ لا يَنْفَعُ مالٌ وَلا بَنُونَ إِلَّا مَنْ أَتَى اللهَ بِقَلْبٍ سَلِيمٍ** (**The day when neither wealth nor children will avail, except him who comes to Allah with a sound heart**) (Ash-Shu'ara: 88-89). When benefit was negated for wealth and children on the Day of Resurrection, it could be imagined that there would be no success or prosperity on that day, since most of what a human benefits from is wealth and children. If these two lose their effect, nothing remains but despair and disappointment. Thus, the answer was given that there is another matter that is entirely beneficial, even if it is not of the same genus as wealth and children: the sound heart (القلب السليم).

What we have mentioned regarding the disjunctive nature of the exception is most in accord with the context of the verse and the fact that His saying: **بِالْباطِلِ** (wrongfully) is a primary restriction in the discourse, similar to His saying, the Exalted: **وَلا تَأْكُلُوا أَمْوالَكُمْ بَيْنَكُمْ بِالْباطِلِ وَتُدْلُوا بِها إِلَى الْحُكَّامِ لِتَأْكُلُوا فَرِيقاً مِنْ أَمْوالِ النَّاسِ** (**Do not eat up your wealth among yourselves wrongfully, nor proffer it to the judges in order to eat up a part of the people’s wealth**) (Al-Baqarah: 188). Accordingly, the verse is not restricted to other sound transactions and legislated matters besides trade that necessitate ownership and permit disposal of wealth, such as gifts (الهبة), settlements (الصلح), rewards (الجعالة), dowries (الأمهار), inheritance (الإرث), and the like.

It is sometimes said that the exception is "connected" (متصل) — *an exception where the excepted item belongs to the same category as the general group* — and that His saying: **بِالْباطِلِ** (wrongfully) is an "explanatory restriction" (قيد توضيحي) brought to clarify the state of the "excepted from" (المستثنى منه) after the exception has been excluded and the prohibition applied. The estimation would be: "Do not eat your wealth among yourselves except that it be trade by mutual consent, for if you eat it through other than the path of trade, it would be a wrongful eating that is prohibited," similar to your saying: "Do not strike the orphan unjustly except for discipline." While this type of usage is permissible and well-known among the people of the language, you have already seen that the disjunctive exception is more in accord with the context of the verse.

It has also been said that the prohibition refers to preventing the spending of wealth on what Allah does not approve of, and trade refers to spending it on what He approves of. It has also been said that the verse used to prohibit the absolute consumption of another's wealth without compensation, and that a man among them would feel constrained about eating at anyone's house after this verse was revealed, until that was abrogated by His saying in Surah al-Nur: **وَلا عَلى أَنْفُسِكُمْ أَنْ تَأْكُلُوا مِنْ بُيُوتِكُمْ** until His saying **أَنْ تَأْكُلُوا جَمِيعاً أَوْ أَشْتاتاً** (**nor [is there any blame] on yourselves if you eat from your own houses... whether you eat together or separately**) (Al-Nur: 61). You have already seen that the verse is far removed from indicating such meanings.

Among the strange interpretations is what some have attempted to justify the "connected" nature of the exception while taking His saying: **بِالْباطِلِ** (wrongfully) as a "precautionary restriction" (قيد احترازي). He said, in essence: What is meant by "wrongful" is consuming wealth without an equivalent compensation. Thus, the "excepted from" clause indicates the prohibition of taking wealth from another wrongfully and without compensation; then trade was excepted from this, despite most of its instances not being free from "wrongfulness," for estimating the compensation with a "just balance" (القسطاس المستقيم) such that it truly equals the value of the item exchanged is very difficult, if not impossible.

Thus, the intent of the exception is to tolerate (التسامح) cases where one of the two compensations is larger than the other, and where the cause of the exchange is the merchant's skill in beautifying his goods and promoting them with flowery speech without cheating, deception, or fraud, as often occurs, among other reasons.

All of this is part of the "wrongfulness" of trade which the Law has permitted out of tolerance and ease for its people. Had this not been permissible in the religion through the exception, no one from its people would have desired trade, and the order of the religious society would have been disrupted. (End of summary).

The falsity of this is apparent from what we have previously established. For "wrongful" (الباطل), as known to the linguists, is that which does not result in its desired effect. The effect of selling and trade is the exchange of two properties and the change of the locus of two ownerships to fulfill the need of each of the two parties for the other's property, such that each obtains what he desires and attains his goal through equivalence (المعادلة). Just as this is achieved through equivalence in the two values, it is also achieved by countering the small with the large if there is joined to the small some desire or fear of the seeker, or some other interest by which, through its addition, it equals the large. The indicator of all this is the occurrence of mutual consent (الرضا) from both sides. When mutual consent occurs, the exchange is not considered wrongful at all.

Furthermore, one who is familiar with the style of the Noble Quran in its explanations would not doubt that it is impossible for the Quran to count a matter as "wrongful" and then command it and guide toward it, while He, the Exalted, said in its description: **يَهْدِي إِلَى الْحَقِّ وَإِلى طَرِيقٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ** (**It guides to the truth and to a straight path**) (Al-Ahqaf: 30). How can that which guides to the truth guide to the wrongful?

Moreover, the implication of this justification is that a human is guided by a true, primordial guidance (اهتداء حق فطري) to his need for the exchange of wealth, then guided by a true, primordial guidance to exchange through balancing, yet what he is guided to is not sufficient to truly fulfill his need until some "wrongfulness" is joined to it. How can the primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — be guided to a matter that is insufficient to fulfill its need and only satisfies part of its concern? And how can the primordial nature be guided to the wrongful? Is the distinction between truth and falsehood in actions anything other than the guidance of the primordial nature or its lack thereof? Therefore, there is no escape for one who makes the exception "connected" but to make His saying: **بِالْباطِلِ** (wrongfully) an explanatory restriction.

Even more wondrous than this justification is what has been narrated from some: that the subtle point (النكتة) in this disjunctive exception is to indicate that everything in this world of trade and its like is of the nature of the "wrongful" because it has no stability or permanence; thus, the rational person should not be occupied by it away from preparing for the Abode of the Hereafter, which is better and more lasting. (End).

This is an error. For even assuming its correctness, it would be a subtle point for a "connected" exception, not a "disjunctive" one. Furthermore, these spiritual realities are only appropriate to be mentioned for verses like His saying, the Exalted: **وَما هذِهِ الْحَياةُ الدُّنْيا إِلَّا لَهْوٌ وَلَعِبٌ وَإِنَّ الدَّارَ الْآخِرَةَ لَهِيَ الْحَيَوانُ** (**The life of this world is nothing but diversion and play, but the abode of the Hereafter is indeed Life**) (Al-Ankabut: 64), and His saying, the Exalted: **ما عِنْدَكُمْ يَنْفَدُ وَما عِنْدَ اللهِ باقٍ** (**What is with you will be exhausted but what is with Allah will remain**) (Al-Nahl: 96), and His saying, the Exalted: **قُلْ ما عِنْدَ اللهِ خَيْرٌ مِنَ اللهْوِ وَمِنَ التِّجارَةِ** (**Say, ‘What is with Allah is better than diversion and trade’**) (Al-Jumu'ah: 11). As for our current context, the application of this subtle point would necessitate the legislation of the "wrongful," and the Quran is far above granting license for the wrongful in any way whatsoever.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلا تَقْتُلُوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ** (and do not kill yourselves). The apparent meaning of the sentence is a prohibition against a person killing himself. However, its pairing with His saying: **لا تَأْكُلُوا أَمْوالَكُمْ بَيْنَكُمْ** (Do not eat your wealth among yourselves)—where the apparent meaning is taking the collective of the believers as a single soul (نفس واحدة) possessing wealth that it must not eat through wrongful means—perhaps intimates or indicates that what is meant by "souls" (الأنفس) is all the souls of the religious society, taken as a single soul where the soul of each part is the soul of the other. In such a society, a person's soul is his own soul, and the soul of another is also his own soul. Thus, if he kills himself or another, he has killed "himself." By this consideration, the sentence—meaning His saying: **وَلا تَقْتُلُوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ** (and do not kill yourselves)—is absolute, encompassing suicide—which is a person killing himself—and a person killing another among the believers.

It may also be possible to benefit from the end of the verse—meaning His saying: **إِنَّ اللهَ كانَ بِكُمْ رَحِيماً** (Indeed Allah is Most Merciful to you)—that the prohibited killing of the soul includes a person throwing himself into the danger of being killed or causing his own destruction leading to his death. This is because justifying the prohibition of killing the soul by "mercy" is more in accord and appropriate for this meaning, as is not hidden. This increases the meaning of the verse in generality and breadth. This very harmony supports that His saying: **إِنَّ اللهَ كانَ بِكُمْ رَحِيماً** (Indeed Allah is Most Merciful to you) is a justification for His saying: **وَلا تَقْتُلُوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ** (and do not kill yourselves) only.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَنْ يَفْعَلْ ذلِكَ عُدْواناً وَظُلْماً** (And whoever does that in transgression and injustice) the verse. Transgression (العدوان) is absolute exceeding of bounds, whether it be permissible and praised or prohibited and blamed. He, the Exalted, said: **فَلا عُدْوانَ إِلَّا عَلَى الظَّالِمِينَ** (**so let there be no hostility except against the wrongdoers**) (Al-Baqarah: 193), and He, the Exalted, said: **وَتَعاوَنُوا عَلَى الْبِرِّ وَالتَّقْوى وَلا تَعاوَنُوا عَلَى الْإِثْمِ وَالْعُدْوانِ** (**Cooperate in piety and God-wariness, but do not cooperate in sin and aggression**) (Al-Ma'idah: 2). Thus, it is broader in its application than injustice (الظلم). Its meaning in the verse is exceeding the limits (الحدود) set by Allah the Exalted. **الإصلاء** (to make one enter/burn) by fire is to burn with it.

In the verse, by virtue of its inclusion of His saying: **ذلِكَ** (that), there is a "shift" (التفات) — *a rhetorical transition in person or address* — from addressing the believers to addressing the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, hinting that whoever among them does that—while they are a single soul, and a single soul should not desire its own destruction—is not among the believers. Thus, the believers are not addressed regarding his punishment; rather, the Messenger is addressed, as he is the one addressed concerning the affairs of the believers and others. Therefore, the speech was built upon generality, as it was said: "And whoever does that in transgression and injustice, We shall soon make him enter [the Fire]," and it did not say: "And whoever among you does that."

The end of the verse—meaning His saying: **وَكانَ ذلِكَ عَلَى اللهِ يَسِيراً** (and that is easy for Allah)—supports that the referent of His saying "that" (ذلك) is the prohibition of killing the souls, based on His saying: **إِنَّ اللهَ كانَ بِكُمْ رَحِيماً** (Indeed Allah is Most Merciful to you) being viewed as a justification for the prohibition of killing only, due to the perfect harmony between the two endings. For the apparent meaning is that Allah only prohibits you from killing yourselves out of mercy and compassion for you; otherwise, His punishment of the one who kills the soul by making him enter the Fire is easy for Him, not difficult. Nevertheless, the return of the justification, as well as the threat, to the sum of the two clauses in the first verse—namely the prohibition of eating wealth wrongfully and the prohibition of killing the soul—is not problematic.

As for the saying of some: that the justification and the threat, or the threat only, refers to all the prohibitions mentioned from the beginning of the Surah until this verse; and likewise the saying of others: that "that" refers to all the prohibitions mentioned from His saying: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا يَحِلُّ لَكُمْ أَنْ تَرِثُوا النِّساءَ كَرْهاً** (**O you who have faith! It is not lawful for you to inherit women against their will**) (An-Nisa: 19) until here, due to the lack of a mentioned punishment for the prohibitions occurring in these verses—this is something for which there is no evidence to consider.

The change of context in His saying: **فسوف نصليه نارا** (We shall soon make him enter a Fire) specifically, from the context of the third person (الغيبة) occurring in His saying: **إِنَّ اللهَ كانَ بِكُمْ رَحِيماً** (Indeed Allah is Most Merciful to you) to the context of the first person (التكلم), follows the shift (الالتفات) occurring in His saying: **ذلِكَ** (that) from addressing the believers to addressing the Messenger. Then, the return to the third person in His saying: **وَكانَ ذلِكَ عَلَى اللهِ يَسِيراً** (and that is easy for Allah) is an intimation of the justification—that is, that it is easy for Him because He is Allah, exalted is His Name.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Majma'*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **بِالْباطِلِ** (wrongfully)—there are two views: one is that it is usury, gambling, short-changing, and injustice. He said: This is what is narrated from Imam al-Baqir عليه‌السلام.

In *Nahj al-Bayan*, from Imam al-Baqir and Imam al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام: that it is gambling, ill-gotten gains (السحت), usury, and [false] oaths.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, from Asbat ibn Salim, who said: I was with Abu Abdillah [al-Sadiq] عليه‌السلام when a man came to him and said: "Inform me about the saying of Allah: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَأْكُلُوا أَمْوالَكُمْ بَيْنَكُمْ بِالْباطِلِ** (O you who have faith! Do not eat your wealth among yourselves wrongfully)." He said: "**He meant by that gambling.**" As for His saying: **وَلا تَقْتُلُوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ** (and do not kill yourselves)—"**He meant by that a man from the Muslims who attacks the polytheists alone, entering their dwellings and being killed; so Allah prohibited them from that.**"

I say: The verse is general regarding eating wrongfully, and the mention of gambling and its like is by way of enumerating instances (المصاديق). Likewise, the interpretation of killing the soul as what was mentioned in the narration is an expansion of the verse, not a restriction to what was mentioned.

In the same source, from Ishaq ibn Abdillah ibn Muhammad ibn Ali ibn al-Husayn, who said: Al-Hasan ibn Zayd narrated to me from his father from Ali ibn Abi Talib عليه‌السلام, who said: **سألت رسول الله صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله عن الجبائر تكون على الكسير ـ كيف يتوضأ صاحبها؟ وكيف يغتسل إذا أجنب؟ قال : يجزيه المسح بالماء عليها في الجنابة والوضوء ، قلت : فإن كان في برد يخاف على نفسه ـ إذا أفرغ الماء على جسده؟ فقرأ رسول الله صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله : وَلا تَقْتُلُوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ إِنَّ اللهَ كانَ بِكُمْ رَحِيماً** — "I asked the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله about the splints (الجبائر) that are upon a person with a fracture—how should he perform ablution (الوضوء)? And how should he perform the ritual bath (الغسل) if he is in a state of major ritual impurity (الجنابة)? He said: 'It suffices him to wipe with water over them in major ritual impurity and ablution.' I said: 'What if it is in cold weather such that he fears for his soul if he pours water over his body?' Then the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله recited: **وَلا تَقْتُلُوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ إِنَّ اللهَ كانَ بِكُمْ رَحِيماً** (and do not kill yourselves; indeed Allah is Most Merciful to you)."

In *al-Faqih*, Imam al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام said: **من قتل نفسه متعمدا فهو في نار جهنم خالدا فيها ، قال الله تعالى : وَلا تَقْتُلُوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ إِنَّ اللهَ كانَ بِكُمْ رَحِيماً ـ وَمَنْ يَفْعَلْ ذلِكَ عُدْواناً وَظُلْماً ـ فَسَوْفَ نُصْلِيهِ ناراً وَكانَ ذلِكَ عَلَى اللهِ يَسِيراً** — "Whoever kills himself intentionally shall be in the fire of Hell, abiding therein forever. Allah the Exalted said: **وَلا تَقْتُلُوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ إِنَّ اللهَ كانَ بِكُمْ رَحِيماً ـ وَمَنْ يَفْعَلْ ذلِكَ عُدْواناً وَظُلْماً ـ فَسَوْفَ نُصْلِيهِ ناراً وَكانَ ذلِكَ عَلَى اللهِ يَسِيراً** (and do not kill yourselves; indeed Allah is Most Merciful to you. And whoever does that in transgression and injustice, We shall soon make him enter a Fire, and that is easy for Allah)."

I say: The narrations, as you see, generalize the meaning of His saying: **وَلا تَقْتُلُوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ** (and do not kill yourselves) the verse, as we inferred previously. There are other narrations with the same meaning as what preceded.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Majah and Ibn al-Mundhir extracted from Ibn Sa'id, who said: The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: **إنما البيع عن تراض** — "Selling is only by mutual consent."

In the same source, Ibn Jarir extracted from Ibn Abbas: That the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله sold to a man, then said to him: "**Choose.**" He said: "I have chosen." He said: "**Thus is the sale.**"

In the same source, al-Bukhari, al-Tirmidhi, and al-Nasa'i extracted from Ibn Umar, who said: The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: **البيعان بالخيار ما لم يفترقا أو يقول أحدهما للآخر : اختر** — "The two parties to a sale have the right of withdrawal (الخيار) as long as they have not separated, or one of them says to the other: 'Choose'."

I say: His saying: "The two parties to a sale have the right of withdrawal as long as they have not separated" is also narrated through Shi'i chains. His saying: "or one of them says to the other: 'Choose'" is to establish the meaning of mutual consent (التراضي).
